varieties.name: Gesha
varieties.note: Floral, jasmine and bergamot-forward; tea-like body. Panama Geisha brought it global fame in 2004.

varieties.name: Pink Bourbon
varieties.note: A pink-cherried Bourbon selection (its colour the result of crossing red- and yellow-fruited Bourbon) prized in Huila and Nariño, Colombia. Low-yielding and prized by specialty growers for a high-toned, complex cup — tropical fruit, intense florals, and a bright, structured acidity.

varieties.name: Sidra
varieties.note: An Ecuadorian-associated selection (often linked to Typica/Bourbon parentage and Nestlé varietal work, a sibling of Typica Mejorado) now increasingly grown in Colombia. Sought after for crystalline cup clarity — floral aromatics, layered stone fruit and citrus, and a tea-like delicacy that has made it a competition favourite.

varieties.name: Wush Wush
varieties.note: Named for the Wush Wush region of southwestern Ethiopia from which the seed stock originates; now a boutique, high-value selection grown in small quantities in Colombia and Central America. Delicate and aromatic — jasmine and tropical florals over juicy stone fruit and a silky body.

varieties.name: SL28
varieties.note: The benchmark Kenyan cultivar, selected at Scott Agricultural Laboratories in the 1930s from a drought-resistant Tanganyika Bourbon-type. Deep-rooted and well suited to lower, drier zones, it is famously susceptible to leaf rust yet endures for its cup: intense blackcurrant, grapefruit and a bright, structured phosphoric acidity that became the global reference for "Kenyan" flavour.

varieties.name: SL34
varieties.note: A Bourbon-Typica-type selection made at Scott Agricultural Laboratories (Scott Labs) in Kenya in the 1930s alongside SL28. More tolerant of higher-altitude, higher-rainfall conditions than SL28 and reliant on deep soils; it gives a full body with deep, syrupy sweetness and the structured citrus-and-blackcurrant acidity that defines the classic Kenyan cup.

varieties.name: Typica
varieties.note: One of the two foundational Arabica lineages (with Bourbon) from which most cultivated varieties descend — carried from Ethiopia/Yemen to India, Java, and the Americas. Tall, low-yielding and rust-susceptible, but prized for an exceptionally clean, sweet, well-balanced cup with gentle citrus and a classic, refined profile.

varieties.name: Bourbon
varieties.note: One of the two ancestral cultivated lineages, via Réunion. Rounded caramel sweetness and balance; parent to Caturra, Pacas, Mundo Novo and many more.

varieties.name: Yellow Bourbon
varieties.note: Yellow-fruited Bourbon selection from Brazil, noted for soft sweetness and a rounder, less acidic cup than red Bourbon.

varieties.name: Caturra
varieties.note: Compact dwarf mutation found in Brazil in the 1930s. Higher-yielding and easier to harvest than Bourbon; bright, clean, citric at altitude.

varieties.name: Pacas
varieties.note: Salvadoran dwarf Bourbon mutation discovered by the Pacas family in 1949. Bright citrus acidity with caramel sweetness; a Pacamara parent.

varieties.name: Villa Sarchi
varieties.note: Costa Rican dwarf Bourbon, wind-resistant and excellent at high altitude. Elegant acidity, intense fruit, structured sweetness.

varieties.name: Laurina
varieties.note: Naturally low-caffeine Bourbon mutation with pointed leaves. Delicate, gentle, low-bitterness cup; rare and pest-prone.

varieties.name: Java
varieties.note: A Typica selection long grown in Indonesia and now Latin America. Clean, sweet, often floral with good structure.

varieties.name: Maragogipe
varieties.note: The 'elephant bean' — a giant-seeded natural mutation of Typica first found near Maragogipe, Bahia, Brazil. Low-yielding but prized for a soft, sweet, elegant, clean cup; a parent of both Pacamara and Maracaturra.

varieties.name: Mundo Novo
varieties.note: Vigorous natural cross found in Brazil in 1943. Heavy body, mild acidity, sweet chocolate and dried-fruit tones.

varieties.name: Pacamara
varieties.note: Large-beaned Salvadoran cross. High-risk, high-reward: explosive flavours from herbal and savoury to papaya and cherry-tomato.

varieties.name: Centroamericano (H1)
varieties.note: F1 hybrid bred for vigour, disease resistance and cup. Complex, sweet and high-yielding; notable at competitions.

varieties.name: Castillo
varieties.note: Colombia's workhorse rust-resistant variety (2005), a multi-line composite. Once doubted on quality; modern processing yields clean nutty-citric-chocolate cups.

varieties.name: Variedad Colombia
varieties.note: Cenicafé's earlier rust-resistant composite (1982), forerunner to Castillo. Productive and hardy with a balanced commercial cup.

varieties.name: Catimor
varieties.note: Disease-resistant hybrid group developed in Portugal. Variable, sometimes earthy/herbal — though high-altitude lots can surprise with clean fruit.

varieties.name: Catuaí
varieties.note: Compact, wind-resistant and highly productive; red- and yellow-fruited lines. Nutty, chocolatey, low-acid, creamy body.

varieties.name: Tabi
varieties.note: Colombian rust-resistant variety (2002); 'Tabi' means 'good'. Tall with large cherries and a sweet, balanced cup closer to Typica/Bourbon.

varieties.name: Marsellesa
varieties.note: Sarchimor-family variety with strong leaf-rust resistance and good cup quality; performs in challenging climates.

varieties.name: Parainema
varieties.note: High-yielding Honduran Sarchimor with rust resistance. Aromatic, medium body, balanced — tropical fruit, citrus and caramel.

varieties.name: Chiroso
varieties.note: Elongated-bean variety from Urrao, Antioquia, genetically tied to Ethiopian landrace. Floral and herbal with juicy citrus — Colombia's 'local Gesha'.

varieties.name: Ombligón
varieties.parents: Uncertain — possible Ethiopian landrace; some link to Caturra/Bourbon/Castillo
varieties.note: Huila variety named for the navel-like dimple on its oblong cherry. Rust-resistant and robust; intense red-fruit, cherry-cola and tropical character, especially under experimental processing.

varieties.name: Wolisho
varieties.note: Named Ethiopian landrace from the Gedeo zone, common in Yirgacheffe lots. Floral, citric and delicate with a tea-like body.

varieties.name: Dega
varieties.note: Ethiopian landrace named after an indigenous tree. Sweet and aromatic, frequently blended into washed Yirgacheffe selections.

varieties.name: So'ngna
varieties.note: Discovered by Fang Cheng-lun at Zou Zhou Yuan among Gesha plantings — ~70 oddly-shaped trees that WCR testing confirmed as an uncategorised Ethiopian-origin landrace. Named 'best' in the Tsou language. High-toned and floral, Gesha-like, with citrus, orange blossom and a tea-like body.

varieties.name: Kent
varieties.parents: Selection from a single tree, Doddengooda Estate (1911)
varieties.note: An Indian selection traced to seed Baba Budan reportedly brought from Yemen in 1670. Selected by L.P. Kent for rust resistance; carried to East Africa and the Pacific. Mild, sweet and balanced.

varieties.name: S795
varieties.note: India's most widely grown selection (also 'Jember' in Indonesia). Carries a touch of Liberica ancestry via S288; soft maple-syrup, caramel and brown-sugar sweetness with a gentle cup.

varieties.name: Cauvery (Catimor)
varieties.parents: Local Catimor sub-variety (Caturra × Timor Hybrid line)
varieties.note: India's locally selected Catimor, bred for leaf-rust resistance and yield. Balanced and low-acid; a backbone variety of Indian Arabica and Monsooned Malabar lots.

varieties.name: K7
varieties.note: A Kenyan Bourbon selection (Scott Labs, released 1936) grown across Kenya and Tanzania. Tolerant of leaf rust and berry disease; large beans with good cup quality at altitude.

varieties.name: N39
varieties.note: A Tanzanian Bourbon selection bred at Lyamungu for yield. A backbone of Kilimanjaro and Mbeya production; bright, winey, blackcurrant-leaning East African character.

varieties.name: Arusha
varieties.parents: A Typica or French Mission type; origins debated
varieties.note: Named for the Mt. Meru area of Tanzania and also widely grown in Papua New Guinea. Classic, clean and balanced with medium body — a workhorse of East African and Pacific highland coffee.

varieties.name: Timor Hybrid (Híbrido de Timor)
varieties.note: A natural Arabica–Robusta cross found on Timor in the 1920s–40s ('Tim Tim'), formally identified in the 1950s. The genetic foundation of every rust-resistant Catimor and Sarchimor. Earthy, mild and nutty with low acidity and Robusta-derived body.

varieties.name: Sarchimor
varieties.note: A group of pure-line cultivars from Villa Sarchi crossed with the Timor Hybrid; parent of Marsellesa and Parainema. Rust-resistant and productive at low-to-mid altitudes, with balanced, mildly sweet cups.

varieties.name: Rume Sudan (Sudan Rume)
varieties.note: A rare wild, disease-resistant Sudanese landrace collected from the Boma Plateau on the Sudan–Ethiopia border, prized as a breeding parent (in Centroamericano, Ruiru 11 and Batian). On its own it can be strikingly complex — intense and syrupy, with bright acidity and fruity, floral character — and is grown in tiny quantities at elite farms.

varieties.name: Ruiru 11
varieties.parents: Catimor (female) × complex male of K7, SL28, N39, Rume Sudan & Timor Hybrid
varieties.note: A compact, hand-pollinated composite released in Kenya in 1985 after the 1968 CBD epidemic. Disease-resistant and high-yielding; a decent, fruit-leaning cup though typically less complex than SL28/SL34.

varieties.name: Batian
varieties.parents: SL28, SL34, Rume Sudan, N39, K7, SL4 & Timor Hybrid
varieties.note: Released in Kenya in 2010 and named for the highest peak of Mt. Kenya. Open-pollinated (so farmers can save seed), disease-resistant and, unlike Ruiru 11, capable of real specialty quality — plum, citrus zest and jasmine above 1,700 m.

varieties.name: Icatú
varieties.parents: C. canephora (chromosome-doubled) × Red Bourbon, backcrossed to Mundo Novo
varieties.note: A tall, high-yielding Brazilian cultivar (released 1993) with Robusta-derived rust resistance bred back toward Arabica quality. Mild, sweet and nutty.

varieties.name: Catucaí
varieties.note: A productive, rust-tolerant Brazilian cross (first selected 1988), in red and yellow forms. Uniform ripening and a balanced cup of sweetness, acidity and body comparable to Catuaí.

varieties.name: Acaiá
varieties.parents: A selection within the Mundo Novo (Bourbon × Typica) line
varieties.note: A Brazilian Mundo Novo selection (1977) with larger beans. Classic Brazilian profile: sweet, nutty and chocolatey with a soft, full body.

varieties.name: Obatã
varieties.note: A compact, rust-resistant Brazilian Sarchimor selection grown for reliable yield; clean and balanced, increasingly shown off in well-processed filter lots.

varieties.name: IHCAFE 90
varieties.note: A Honduran Catimor released in the 1980s that underpinned Honduras's rise as Central America's biggest exporter. Reliable and productive, though now eclipsed on cup quality by newer hybrids.

varieties.name: Lempira
varieties.note: Alongside Parainema, one of Honduras's two most widely planted varieties. High-yielding and once strongly rust-resistant, with a balanced, mild cup; some rust resistance has since broken down.

varieties.name: Anacafe 14
varieties.note: A serendipitous Guatemalan cross from the 1980s (Pacamara or Maragogipe with a Timor Hybrid line). Rust-resistant and drought-tolerant with surprisingly good, sweet, balanced cup quality.

varieties.name: Cenicafé 1
varieties.note: A compact, highly productive Colombian variety from the FNC's Cenicafé program, bred for rust and CBD resistance and broad adaptability — a successor line to Castillo.

varieties.name: Papayo
varieties.parents: Once thought a Caturra mutant; WCR ties it to Ethiopian landraces
varieties.note: A rare Huila variety with a distinctive elongated, papaya-like cherry (hence the name). Genetic testing links it to Ethiopian landraces rather than Caturra; sweet and floral when well grown.

varieties.name: JARC 74110
varieties.parents: Selected from wild trees, Metu-Bishari forest
varieties.note: A compact, CBD-resistant selection developed by the Jimma Agricultural Research Center (released 1979) from forest trees in Illubabor. Widely planted in southern Ethiopia; clean, sweet, floral washed profiles.

varieties.name: JARC 74112
varieties.parents: Selected from wild trees, Metu-Bishari forest
varieties.note: A sister JARC selection to 74110, chosen for CBD resistance and yield. Common across Yirgacheffe and Guji; bright, floral and fruit-forward in the cup.

varieties.name: Sigarar Utang
varieties.note: A widely planted Sumatran variety whose name translates roughly as 'pay off debt quickly' — it fruits almost year-round. Sweet and full-bodied, a backbone of Aceh and North Sumatra production.

varieties.name: Ateng
varieties.note: A common Indonesian name (from Aceh Tengah) for locally grown Catimor types. Hardy and productive; heavy-bodied, earthy-herbal wet-hulled Sumatran character.

varieties.name: Eugenioides
varieties.parents: Wild species; a parent of Arabica (with Robusta)
varieties.note: A separate species and one of Arabica's two parents. Remarkably low caffeine and intensely sweet — sesame, toasted marshmallow, lemon drop; soft, silky and low-acid. Rare and very expensive.

varieties.name: Robusta
varieties.parents: Distinct species; a parent of Arabica
varieties.note: The second commercial species, ~40% of world output. High caffeine, hardy, heavy-bodied; harsh as commodity but capable of clean cocoa/nutty fine-Robusta lots.

varieties.name: Nganda
varieties.parents: A cultivar group of C. canephora
varieties.note: An Ugandan Robusta cultivar group (the other main one being 'Robusta' proper). Often spreading/bushy in habit; used for hardy, heavy-bodied lots and as rootstock.

varieties.name: Kouillou (Conilon)
varieties.parents: A cultivar group of C. canephora
varieties.note: A C. canephora group known as Conilon in Brazil, where it dominates low-altitude Robusta production. Earthy, woody and full-bodied; the workhorse of commercial Robusta.

varieties.name: Liberica
varieties.note: Large asymmetric 'hooked' beans on towering trees. Bold and aromatic — jackfruit, tropical fruit, floral over smoky-woody depth. Known as Barako in the Philippines.

varieties.name: Excelsa
varieties.parents: Reclassified as a variety of Liberica
varieties.note: Lighter and brighter than classic Liberica: tart berry, tamarind and tropical tones over a faint smoky backnote. Mostly Southeast Asian; prized in blends and as a single origin.

varieties.name: Racemosa
varieties.note: A rare drought-tolerant species from the coastal forest belt of Mozambique and northern KwaZulu-Natal — endemic to under 150 km². Naturally low caffeine (~0.8%), with tiny beans, fast 3-month ripening and a delicate, sweet, citrus-and-floral cup. Production measured in kilograms; a climate-resilience hope for coffee's future.

varieties.name: Stenophylla
varieties.parents: Distinct species; rediscovered in the wild in 2018
varieties.note: A West African species once cultivated, then lost to commerce in the 20th century and rediscovered wild in Sierra Leone in 2018. Heat-tolerant yet — unusually — reported to taste remarkably Arabica-like, with sweetness, complexity and a delicate fruit-and-floral character. A key climate-adaptation candidate.

varieties.name: Purple Caturra
varieties.parents: A Caturra whose cherries ripen deep purple
varieties.note: A Caturra type whose cherries ripen to a deep purple. Nearly proprietary to Finca Monteblanco (Rodrigo Sánchez), where it was rediscovered among 1980s plantings; higher Brix and a distinctive sweet, dark-fruited cup, used for special processing.

varieties.name: Tekisic
varieties.note: An improved Bourbon selected by the Salvadoran Institute for Coffee Research (ISIC) from 1949, released 1977. Ripens early, very good cup at altitude; makes up a large share of El Salvador production. Susceptible to leaf rust.

varieties.name: Cuscatleco
varieties.note: A Sarchimor-type developed by PROCAFÉ in El Salvador from the T5296 line. Rust-resistant, suited to medium altitude.

varieties.name: Catisic
varieties.note: A leaf-rust-resistant Catimor-type developed in El Salvador (PROCAFÉ/ISIC), released around 1977. A working, disease-resistant cultivar.

varieties.name: Pache Común
varieties.note: A dwarf mutation of Typica first observed at Finca El Brito, Santa Rosa, Guatemala. Compact like Caturra; clean, balanced Typica cup.

varieties.name: Pache Colís
varieties.note: A compact Guatemalan variety, a hybrid of Caturra and Pache Común — shorter than either parent. Clean, well-balanced Typica-family character.

varieties.name: Villalobos
varieties.note: A Typica-group selection associated with Costa Rica; tall plant valued for cup quality and adaptability at altitude.

varieties.name: Starmaya
varieties.parents: Marsellesa × wild Ethiopian (Sudan-derived) male sterile line
varieties.note: The first F1 hybrid that can be propagated by seed (not only clones), developed by CIRAD with ECOM. Rust-resistant with good cup potential.

varieties.name: Maracaturra
varieties.note: A cross of Maragogipe and Caturra producing very large beans on a semi-dwarf plant; prized in competitions for big, expressive cups.

varieties.name: Oro Azteca
varieties.note: A leaf-rust-resistant Catimor-type developed in Mexico (INIFAP), released 1990s; widely planted for durability.

varieties.name: Pluma Hidalgo
varieties.note: A Typica progeny long grown around Pluma Hidalgo, Oaxaca; classic mild, sweet, clean Typica profile.

varieties.name: Costa Rica 95
varieties.note: A Sarchimor-derived rust-resistant variety released by ICAFE in Costa Rica (named for its 1995 release). Vigorous, medium-altitude workhorse.

varieties.name: Tupi
varieties.note: A Sarchimor-line cultivar developed by IAC/EPAMIG in Brazil; rust-resistant, compact, good yields.

varieties.name: Rubi
varieties.note: A Brazilian cultivar (Rubi MG 1192) from a Mundo Novo × Catuaí cross; productive, good cup, widely planted in Minas Gerais.

varieties.name: Ouro Verde
varieties.note: A Brazilian IAC cultivar from the Mundo Novo × Catuaí lineage; balanced productivity and cup, suited to Brazilian conditions.

varieties.name: Catiguá MG2
varieties.note: A Brazilian EPAMIG cultivar with leaf-rust resistance and good cup potential; part of the modern Minas Gerais portfolio.

varieties.name: JARC 74140
varieties.note: A JARC selection from the 1974 Illubabor/Metu collections, chosen for coffee berry disease resistance. Good cup in matched zones.

varieties.name: JARC 74148
varieties.note: A JARC landrace selection (1974 series) bred for disease resistance; an Ethiopian heirloom subvariety.

varieties.name: JARC 74158
varieties.note: A widely planted JARC selection noted for leaf-rust resistance and high-altitude adaptability; expressive, fruit-forward cups (e.g. Hamasho naturals).

varieties.name: JARC 74165
varieties.note: A JARC heirloom selection (1974 series) catalogued for disease resistance; common in Sidama/Guji plantings.

varieties.name: JARC 75227
varieties.note: A JARC selection from the 1975 collections; an Ethiopian heirloom subvariety grown across the south-west.

varieties.name: Bishari
varieties.note: An Ethiopian heirloom subvariety from Bishari village in Illubabor — the same source population behind several JARC selections (e.g. 74110).

varieties.name: Merdacheriko
varieties.note: An Ethiopian heirloom subvariety catalogued among the local landraces; grown in the south, prized for clarity and florals.

varieties.name: Mibirizi
varieties.note: A Bourbon-derived variety named for Mibirizi, Rwanda, where it was selected in the 1930s; widespread across Rwanda, Burundi and the Great Lakes region.

varieties.name: Jackson
varieties.note: A Bourbon selection (Jackson 2/1257) long grown in Rwanda, Burundi and East Africa; classic Bourbon sweetness and acidity.

varieties.name: French Mission
varieties.note: A name for the Bourbon plants spread through East Africa by French missionaries in the late 1800s; foundational to Kenyan and Tanzanian Bourbon stock.

varieties.name: Sln. 9
varieties.note: An Indian Arabica selection (Selection 9) from a cross involving an Ethiopian type and Timor Hybrid; valued in Indian estates for cup and rust resistance.

varieties.name: BRS 2299
varieties.note: One of Embrapa’s individually registered Amazonian Robusta clones, selected in Rondônia for vigour, yield and rust resistance.

varieties.name: BRS 3210
varieties.note: An Embrapa "Robusta Amazônico" clonal hybrid cultivar for the western Amazon, characterised individually for yield, bean size and quality.

varieties.name: Apoatã (IAC 2258)
varieties.note: A productive Brazilian Robusta cultivar released by IAC in 1987; nematode-resistant and widely used as a rootstock as well as for production.

varieties.name: Yellow Catucaí
varieties.note: A high-yielding, rust-tolerant Brazilian cultivar from the Catucaí line (a Catuaí × Icatu natural cross), ripening yellow. Common in Minas Gerais specialty lots, especially fermentation-focused ones.

varieties.name: Topázio
varieties.note: A Brazilian cultivar (Topázio MG-1190) from a Mundo Novo × Catuaí cross, ripening yellow. Valued for sweetness and consistency; used in many specialty and experimentally-fermented Minas lots.

varieties.name: Esperanza (F1)
varieties.note: An F1 hybrid created by CATIE/CIRAD/World Coffee Research crossing rust-resistant Sarchimor T-5296 with Ethiopian landrace ET25. Rare at commercial scale; grown notably at Aquiares Estate. Clean, sweet, floral-leaning cup.

varieties.name: SL9 ("Gesha Inca")
varieties.parents: Scott Laboratories selection; genetically Gesha-like / Ethiopian landrace
varieties.note: A Gesha-like variety grown in the Cusco highlands of Peru, colloquially called "Gesha Inca." Genetic testing suggests it is close to an old Scott Laboratories (SL) selection; Gesha-like in stature and a berry-forward, floral, Ethiopian-landrace cup profile.

varieties.name: Las Tolas
varieties.parents: Undetermined; Bourbon-like in habit and cup
varieties.note: A variety colloquially known as Las Tolas, grown in Pichincha, Ecuador (notably at Terrazas del Pisque). Bourbon-like in format with dark-fruit, dried-berry and cacao character; its precise lineage is not formally established.

varieties.name: Mejorado
varieties.parents: Reportedly a natural Bourbon × Ethiopian-landrace cross found in Ecuador
varieties.note: An Ecuadorian variety (“improved”) associated with high-end farms such as Finca Soledad; thought to be a natural cross with Ethiopian-landrace character, prized for floral, tropical cups.

varieties.name: Aruzi
varieties.note: A rare variety name encountered in Huila, Colombia (e.g. Victor Bonilla, Finca La Chorrera). Limited public documentation of its lineage.

varieties.name: Caturrón
varieties.parents: A large-seeded selection associated with Caturra
varieties.note: A "Caturrón" (large-bean Caturra-type) selection grown in Huila, Colombia, e.g. by Café Ticuna in Garzón.

varieties.name: Striped Red Bourbon
varieties.note: A striped-fruit selection of Red Bourbon grown in Huila, Colombia (e.g. Elkin Guzmán, Finca El Mirador).

varieties.name: Pink Pacamara
varieties.note: A pink-cherried selection of Pacamara, the large-beaned Salvadoran cross of Pacas and Maragogipe. Grown in small quantities (notably at Los Pirineos in El Salvador) and valued for combining Pacamara’s big body and structure with delicate floral, stone-fruit and pear-like sweetness.

varieties.name: Red Catuai
varieties.note: The red-cherry form of Catuai, a compact high-yielding Brazilian cultivar widely grown across Latin America (e.g. Bolivia).

varieties.name: Kurume
varieties.note: A small-cherry, small-leaf Ethiopian landrace type prized in Yirgacheffe/Gedeo for floral, tea-like clarity; often grown among mixed garden varieties.

varieties.name: Ethiopian Landrace (mixed heirloom)
varieties.note: The catch-all term for the thousands of indigenous heirloom Arabica types grown as mixed "garden coffee" across Ethiopia; not a single cultivar but a diverse landrace population.

varieties.name: San Pacho (Bourbon)
varieties.note: A Salvadoran Bourbon selection grown in Apaneca-Ilamatepec; associated with producer Jose Enrique Gutierrez (El Limo).

varieties.name: Sigararutang (Sigarar Utang)
varieties.note: A widely planted Indonesian Arabica line, high-yielding and continuously fruiting ("Sigarar Utang" loosely means "pay debts immediately"); Catimor-derived, common across Sumatra and Java.

varieties.name: Ateng Super
varieties.note: An Indonesian Catimor-type selection ("Ateng" from Aceh + Tengah), valued for disease resistance and yield in Sumatra and Java.

varieties.name: Tim Tim (Timor)
varieties.note: "Tim Tim" (Timor-Timur) is the Indonesian name for Hibrido de Timor types — natural Arabica × Robusta hybrids brought from Timor, foundational to many Indonesian plantings.

varieties.name: Abyssinia (Abesinia)
varieties.note: An older Ethiopian-origin Arabica selection long grown in Sumatra/Aceh; valued for cup quality among the region's mixed garden plantings.

varieties.name: Gayo
varieties.note: A locally named Arabica selection from the Gayo highlands of Aceh, Sumatra; part of the mixed-variety smallholder plantings typical of the region.

varieties.name: Bor Bor
varieties.note: A locally named Sumatran Arabica selection grown among Aceh and Gayo highland smallholdings. Part of the mixed-variety garden plantings typical of the region, contributing to the herbal, sweet, full-bodied character of Gayo coffees.

varieties.name: Andung Sari (Andungsari)
varieties.note: An Indonesian Arabica research line (Andung Sari) derived from Catimor stock, released for disease resistance and adaptability; planted in Kerinci and elsewhere.

varieties.parents: Selected from indigenous Ethiopian landrace by the Jimma Agricultural Research Center
varieties.note: A JARC-released improved selection of indigenous Ethiopian landrace, planted notably at Israel Degfa's Ana Sora estate in Guji; valued for disease resistance and a bright, fruity, floral cup at high altitude.

varieties.name: Gori Gesha
varieties.parents: Wild heirloom from the Gori Gesha forest
varieties.note: An heirloom Gesha selection harvested from the Gori Gesha forest near Gesha Village — the forest genetically identified as the collection site of the Panamanian Geisha. Replicates the wild forest population diversity.

varieties.name: Illubabor Forest
varieties.parents: Selected from a 1974 Illubabor coffee-forest expedition
varieties.note: A disease-resistant Ethiopian landrace selection made from a 1974 expedition to the Illubabor coffee forest; grown at Gesha Village alongside its Gesha types.

farms.name: Finca El Diviso
farms.note: Pitalito, Huila. Third-generation family farm turned competition powerhouse through precision fermentation — oxidation, anaerobic steps, cherry lixiviates and thermal shock. Famed for Ombligón and exotic Bourbons (Ají, Rosado).
farms.notable: A pioneer of thermal-shock and advanced fermentation, recognised at world barista competitions.

farms.name: Granja Paraíso 92
farms.note: Piendamó, Cauca. Set a Colombian auction record in 2019. On-site microbiology and quality labs, 32+ exotic varietals, multi-stage controlled fermentation. Part of the Varietales Finos project with La Macarena and Las Brisas.
farms.notable: Set a Colombian auction record in 2019.

farms.name: Finca Las Flores
farms.note: Pitalito, Huila. Vergara's farm, partnered with the Lassos in the El Diviso venture; an early planter of exotic varietals and extended experimental fermentations.

farms.name: Finca Santa Mónica
farms.note: Quindío. Arcila-family farm known for clean, well-built classic and experimental lots; widely roasted internationally.

farms.name: Finca El Mirador / La Quebraditas
farms.note: Worked on innovative Cauca farms before founding a family farm with partner Luz Ángela; precision thermal-shock and yeast-inoculated fermentation. Competition Ombligón.

farms.name: Caicedo / Urrao Chiroso growers
farms.note: Urrao, Antioquia. Carmen Montoya's Chiroso won the 2014 Colombian Cup of Excellence, putting the variety — and the Caicedo growing community — on the map.
farms.notable: Carmen Montoya's Chiroso won the 2014 Colombian Cup of Excellence, putting the variety — and the Caicedo growing community — on the map.

farms.name: Danche (SNAP) Washing Station
farms.note: Worka Chelbessa, Gedeb. SNAP's celebrated Danche site (est. 2019), ceramic fermentation tanks for clarity. Among the most sought-after Yirgacheffe washing stations in the world; washed, natural and experimental lots.
farms.notable: SNAP Specialty Coffee’s celebrated Danche site, a benchmark Yirgacheffe-area name.

farms.name: Worka Chelchele Washing Station
farms.note: Banko Chelchele, Gedeb. Built 2021 with 12 fermentation tanks and 250+ raised beds; gathers cherry from ~400 smallholders. Known for explosive, jammy Gedeb cup profiles.

farms.name: Gesha Village Estate
farms.note: Bench Maji, near the Gori Gesha forest where Gesha originated. Large estate planted from local Gesha selections; its annual Gesha Village Auction is a global benchmark.
farms.notable: The landmark Ethiopian estate beside the Gori Gesha forest, source of much of the world's Gesha lineage.

farms.name: Hacienda La Esmeralda
farms.note: Boquete. The farm that launched the modern Gesha era at the 2004 Best of Panama. Swept all three categories at the 2025 BoP — a first in 29 years — with coffees from its high-altitude 'Nido' plot.
farms.notable: The farm that launched the modern Gesha era at the 2004 Best of Panama.

farms.name: Elida Estate (Lamastus Family Estates)
farms.note: Boquete, on Barú Volcano. A coffee legacy since 1918 across Elida, El Burro and Luito estates; repeated record-breaking Gesha auction prices. Intensely sweet, honey-forward naturals.
farms.notable: Repeated record-breaking Gesha auction prices from the Lamastus family.

farms.name: Ninety Plus Gesha Estates
farms.note: Volcán (and Ethiopia). Producer-roaster pushing extreme experimental processing; private-auction Geshas have sold for record per-kilo prices in Dubai.
farms.notable: Private-auction Geshas have sold for record per-kilo prices.

farms.name: Finca Deborah
farms.note: Volcán, high on Barú. Single-variety Gesha specialist known for delicate, champagne-like, silky cups and meticulous processing.

farms.name: Finca San Rafael (Pacas family)
farms.note: Santa Ana, El Salvador. The estate where the Pacas mutation was discovered in 1949; a long lineage of Bourbon, Pacas and Pacamara.

farms.name: Finca Deborah (Marcala)
farms.note: Marcala, Honduras. Third-generation farm noted for sustainable cultivation and a range including Parainema, Icatú, Catuaí, Gesha and Pacamara.

farms.name: Finca Takesi
farms.note: Yanacachi, Bolivia. Extremely high-altitude estate along an ancient Inca trail; rare, sought-after Bolivian Gesha.

farms.name: Finca Santa Elisa
farms.note: Acatenango, Guatemala. High-altitude estate producing high-toned, sweet-tart Gesha and classic Guatemalan profiles.

farms.name: Gatomboya Factory
farms.note: Nyeri. One of Kenya's most decorated washing stations (Barichu FCS), repeatedly among the world's top-scoring lots. Quintessential blackcurrant-and-citrus Nyeri profile.
farms.notable: One of Kenya's most decorated washing stations (Barichu FCS), repeatedly among the world's top-scoring lots.

farms.name: Kii Factory
farms.note: Kirinyaga, at the foot of Mt. Kenya (built 1995). One of Rungeto FCS's three factories; complex, clean cups with jasmine, bergamot and stone fruit over a blackcurrant core.
farms.notable: One of Kenya's most sought-after factories, a benchmark for SL-28 clarity.

farms.name: Ndaroini Factory
farms.note: Mathira, Nyeri. Part of Gikanda Society (with Gichathaini and Kangocho); a benchmark Nyeri washing station sourced direct by several importers.

farms.name: Daterra Farms
farms.note: Patrocínio, Cerrado Mineiro. A pioneering sustainability-certified estate with an on-farm experimentation 'Masterpieces' lab; velvet-chocolate to bright-floral profiles via the Penta Process.
farms.notable: A pioneering, sustainability-certified Brazilian estate whose on-farm “Masterpiece” lab releases experimental micro-lots that regularly set auction records.

farms.name: Carmo Coffees
farms.note: Carmo de Minas, Mantiqueira de Minas. An influential producer-exporter network behind many award-winning Brazilian micro-lots; sweet, balanced, chocolate-and-stone-fruit cups.

farms.name: Las Lajas Micromill
farms.note: Sabanilla, Central Valley. Organic third-generation micromill that pioneered Costa Rican naturals and honeys — 'Perla Negra', 'Alma Negra', and the yellow/red/black honey spectrum.
farms.notable: An organic, third-generation Costa Rican micromill that pioneered the country’s natural and honey processes — home of the celebrated Perla Negra, Alma Negra and Diamante Negro lots.

farms.name: COOPAC (Kivu)
farms.note: Lake Kivu, Western Rwanda. A long-running, Fairtrade-certified cooperative of Bourbon-type smallholders; clean, balanced, attractively fruited cups in the East African style.

farms.name: Kayanza Washing Stations
farms.note: Kayanza, northern Burundi. High-altitude Bourbon delivered to hillside washing stations; bright, juicy, floral lots when free of the region's 'potato defect'.

farms.name: Qima Coffee
farms.note: Haraz and across Yemen. Yemen's largest specialty exporter, working cherry-level with 2,600+ farmers and researching the country's vast heirloom genetics. Deep, winey, dried-fruit and cocoa Mokha character.
farms.notable: Yemen's leading specialty exporter and a multiple international award winner.

farms.name: Klasik Beans (Puntang)
farms.note: Mt. Puntang, West Java. A cooperative reforesting with coffee and turning out clean, delicate lots that counter Indonesia's heavy-earthy reputation.

farms.name: Gayo Highlands cooperatives
farms.note: Gayo, Aceh, Sumatra. Wet-hulled (Giling Basah) smallholder coffee giving the full-bodied, low-acid, earthy-herbal profile that defines Sumatran Arabica.

farms.name: Baarbara Estate
farms.note: Bababudangiri, Chikmagalur, Karnataka — one of India’s oldest and most respected estates, run by the Indavara family since 1896. Dense, multi-tier shade over Selection 795 and other Indian Arabicas, intercropped with black pepper; a haven for tigers, wild boar and birdlife. Known for clean, low-acid, sweet and gently spiced cups, and certified for sustainability and quality.
farms.notable: One of Karnataka’s oldest and most respected coffee farms, growing shade-grown Selection 795 in the Bababudangiri hills since 1896.

farms.name: Finca Nueva Linda
farms.note: Chiapas, Mexico. A 'legendary producer' family farm; clean, sweet, balanced highland cups from classic varieties.
farms.notable: A family farm regarded as a legendary Guatemalan producer of clean, classic high-grown coffee.

farms.name: CENFROCAFE Cooperative
farms.note: Jaén, Cajamarca, northern Peru — founded in 1999 with 220 farmers and now one of Latin America’s largest and best-organised cooperatives, serving 2,000+ smallholder families across twelve districts. Cajamarca produces the bulk of Peru’s best-cupping coffee, and CENFROCAFE members regularly reach the finals of the Peru Cup of Excellence. Mostly organic-certified washed Bourbon, Caturra, Typica and Catimor.
farms.notable: One of Latin America’s largest and best-organised cooperatives, uniting 2,000+ Cajamarca smallholders, with members regularly placing in Peru’s Cup of Excellence.

farms.name: Zou Zhou Yuan Estate
farms.note: Lalauya, Alishan. Taiwan's most famous farm, run by an indigenous Tsou family; multiple 'Best of Taiwan' wins and record auction prices. Home of the So'ngna variety, discovered here. Natural Gesha with mango purée, rose and sugarcane.
farms.notable: Taiwan’s most famous coffee farm, run by an indigenous Tsou family and a fixture at international auctions.

farms.name: Jing Long Coffee Manor
farms.note: Alishan, Chiayi (est. 2021). A tea-family farm turned to coffee; CO₂-infused anaerobic-washed Gesha showcasing Taiwan's experimental edge.

farms.name: Songyue Coffee Estate
farms.note: Caoling, Gukeng — 'Taiwan's coffee capital'. Gold-award estate experimenting with grafting coffee onto native shrubs for climate resilience; record auction prices.
farms.notable: Known for record Taiwanese auction prices.

farms.name: Lujiangba (Baoshan) estates
farms.note: Lujiangba valley, Baoshan — the cradle of Chinese Arabica, with century-old Typica and Bourbon trees. Volcanic soils and föhn winds; increasingly experimental Geisha and red-wine naturals.
farms.notable: The cradle of Chinese Arabica, with century-old Typica and Bourbon trees.

farms.name: Xiaowazi Coffee Manor
farms.note: Simao District, Pu'er, Yunnan — beside the Nandao River, about 17 km from the city. A roughly 20-hectare manor founded by Liao Xiugui, a local pioneer who began growing coffee in the late 1980s, now home to more than 40 coffee varieties. In 2022 it became one of the first estates authorised to use the “Pu'er Coffee” geographical indication, and is a benchmark for the region’s shift from commodity Catimor toward traceable specialty lots.
farms.notable: A pioneering 20-hectare Pu'er estate with 40+ coffee varieties, among the first authorised to use the “Pu'er Coffee” geographical indication (2022).

farms.name: Doi Chang
farms.note: Doi Chang, Chiang Rai. A flagship Thai origin grown by Akha and Lisu communities; clean, sweet, chocolate-and-citrus highland Arabica that put Thai coffee on the specialty map.

farms.name: Sigri Estate
farms.note: Wahgi Valley, Western Highlands (est. 1950s). One of PNG's most renowned estates, growing Jamaica-derived Blue Mountain Typica and Arusha; clean, balanced, fruit-and-chocolate cups with a syrupy body.
farms.notable: One of Papua New Guinea’s most renowned estates, growing Jamaica-derived Blue Mountain Typica and Arusha at altitude in the Wahgi Valley.

farms.name: Kilimanjaro / Mbeya cooperatives
farms.note: Slopes of Kilimanjaro and the southern Mbeya highlands. N39, K7, Kent and Arusha from smallholders; bright, winey, blackcurrant-leaning cups and the prized Tanzania Peaberry.

farms.name: Sipi Falls (Mt. Elgon)
farms.note: Mt. Elgon, eastern Uganda. Though Uganda is mostly Robusta, the Bugisu Arabica from around Sipi Falls is clean, bright and fruity — the country's specialty highlight.

farms.name: Greenwell Farms
farms.people: Greenwell family (founded by Henry Nicholas Greenwell)
farms.note: Kealakekua, the “heart of Kona,” on the slopes of Hualālai and Mauna Loa, Big Island. Founded in 1850 by Henry Nicholas Greenwell and family-run for five generations, it earned a Recognition Diploma at the 1873 Vienna World’s Fair and remains one of Kona’s oldest and largest estates. Grows the heirloom Kona Typica and runs its own varietal breeding program (origin of the Mamo selection); famously smooth, sweet and low in acidity.
farms.notable: A landmark Kona estate cultivating 100% Kona coffee since 1850, awarded at the 1873 Vienna World’s Fair.

farms.name: Clifton Mount Estate
farms.note: Eastern slope of Catherine’s Peak, Newcastle, St. Andrew, Jamaica — at around 1,300 m in the heart of the Blue Mountains. Owned by the Sharp family since 1977 and fully vertically integrated (the only Jamaican plantation controlling every step from seedling to dry-milling), it is the island’s only Rainforest Alliance-certified estate. Grows classic Blue Mountain Typica, prized for an exquisitely balanced, smooth cup, and famous for its rare peaberry.
farms.notable: The only Rainforest Alliance-certified Jamaican estate, widely regarded as the finest producer of Blue Mountain coffee.

farms.name: My Liberica
farms.note: Johor, Malaysia. A pioneering specialty-Liberica producer-roaster elevating a once-overlooked species; jackfruit, floral and smoky-woody character. Used by a WBC finalist in 2021.
farms.notable: A pioneering Malaysian specialty-Liberica producer-roaster elevating a long-overlooked coffee species into the specialty conversation.

farms.name: Finca La Bohemia
farms.people: Raquel Lasso Muñoz / Carlos Alberto Belalcázar
farms.note: Nariño. Won 1st place in the 2022 Colombia Cup of Excellence (washed Gesha, ~90.6) and placed again in 2023. The family rebuilt depleted land over decades, moving from Caturra to Colombia (for rust) and planting Gesha in 2019.
farms.notable: Won 1st place in the 2022 Colombia Cup of Excellence (washed Gesha, ~90.6) and placed again in 2023.

farms.name: Finca El Vergel (Vergel Estate)
farms.note: Líbano, Tolima. An internationally roasted experimental-processing estate (Vergel Estate / Forest Coffee) known for co-fermentation and exotic-fruit-forward lots; a fixture in modern Colombian specialty and competition circuits.
farms.notable: The Bayter family's Tolima estate, a pioneer of koji and cascara fermentation feeding world competitors.

farms.name: La Palma y El Tucán
farms.note: Zipacón, Cundinamarca. An avant-garde estate famous for its Neighbours & Crops model and pioneering controlled-fermentation lots; a frequent presence in barista-championship sourcing and high-end auctions.
farms.notable: An avant-garde Colombian estate famous for its “Neighbours & Crops” sourcing model and its pioneering, precisely controlled fermentation lots.

farms.name: Cerro Azul (Granja La Esperanza)
farms.note: Trujillo, Valle del Cauca. The elite Geisha site of the historic Granja La Esperanza group, whose Cerro Azul Geisha is one of Colombia’s most awarded and sought-after competition coffees worldwide.
farms.notable: The elite Geisha site of the historic Granja La Esperanza group, whose Cerro Azul Geisha is among Colombia’s most awarded and sought-after coffees.

farms.name: Hacienda San Alberto
farms.note: Buenavista, Quindío. A premium estate brand known for a triple-selection of the cherry and multiple national quality awards; one of Colombia’s best-known specialty marques.
farms.notable: One of Colombia’s best-known specialty marques.

farms.name: Finca El Zafiro
farms.note: Popayán, Cauca. A high-altitude, volcanic-soil farm respected for clean, exotic competition-grade lots and experimental fermentations.

farms.name: La Inmaculada (Inmaculada Coffee Farms)
farms.note: Cauca / Valle. A large, internationally recognised producer-exporter combining estate production with smallholder partnerships; a long-standing specialty and competition supplier.

farms.name: Finca Sophia
farms.people: Willem Boot, David Pohl & Kelly Hartmann
farms.note: Nueva Suiza, Volcán, near Cerro Punta — one of the highest farms in Panama (>2,000 m). Founded 2008 as an experimental high-altitude Geisha farm; multiple Best of Panama wins incl. 1st place Washed Geisha (2017, 2020). Famed for extreme-altitude clarity and florality.
farms.notable: Multiple Best of Panama wins for its high-altitude Gesha.

farms.name: Finca Hartmann
farms.note: Santa Clara, near the Parque Internacional La Amistad. A multi-generational, biodiversity-focused estate; consistent Best of Panama performer and source of the celebrated Black Jaguar Geisha (2022 BoP winner).
farms.notable: Consistent Best of Panama performer and source of the celebrated Black Jaguar Geisha (2022 BoP winner).

farms.name: Carmen Estate
farms.note: Paso Ancho, Volcán. Over 50 years of coffee-growing; a premier Geisha estate with repeated Best of Panama podiums (incl. 2nd place Geisha Natural 2024 for Carmen Geisha Especial Natural).
farms.notable: A premier Geisha estate with repeated Best of Panama podiums.

farms.name: Janson Coffee Farm
farms.note: Volcán/Tisingal slopes. Founded 1941; solar-powered, pesticide-free estate now run by the fourth generation. A regular Best of Panama and World Brewers Cup name; Janson Geisha is widely exported.
farms.notable: A regular Best of Panama placer and World Brewers Cup name, family-run on the slopes of Volcán in Chiriquí.

farms.name: Finca La Mula
farms.note: Boquete. Willem Boot’s single-variety Geisha farm; a recurring top-scoring Best of Panama lot known for intense, perfumed high-altitude Geisha.
farms.notable: A recurring top-scoring Best of Panama lot known for intense, perfumed high-altitude Geisha.

farms.name: Finca Auromar
farms.note: Nueva Suiza, Volcán (est. 2008). A high-elevation estate producing washed, natural and experimental Geisha, plus the local Guayabita; a consistent BoP and auction presence.
farms.notable: A consistent BoP and auction presence.

farms.name: Don Pepe Estate
farms.note: Volcán Barú foothills. Opened 1898 — the oldest coffee farm in the region and the first in Panama to specialise in coffee; now a fifth-generation Geisha producer across natural, honey and washed.

farms.name: Finca Lérida
farms.note: Boquete (est. 1924). Exported the first-ever shipment of Panamanian coffee (to Germany, 1929). A historic 900-acre estate growing Geisha and classic varieties; a long-standing specialty name.

farms.name: Black Moon Farm
farms.note: Volcán. A regenerative-farming estate; won the Best of Panama 2024 Varietals category with its Black Moon Constellation Chiroso lot, and continues to place with Geisha.
farms.notable: Won the Best of Panama 2024 Varietals category with its Black Moon Constellation Chiroso lot, and continues to place with Geisha.

farms.name: Adaura Coffee (Finca Loreyne)
farms.note: Boquete. Took 1st place Washed Geisha at Best of Panama 2024 with the Kaizen lot (also a 2024 auction record near US0,002/kg). A rising precision-processing producer.
farms.notable: Took 1st place Washed Geisha at Best of Panama 2024 with the Kaizen lot (also a 2024 auction record near US0,002/kg).

farms.name: Altieri Coffee (Mima Estate)
farms.note: Volcán. A newer estate making a name at Best of Panama (e.g. 3rd place Geisha Natural 2024 with the Satori DFC lot); innovative processing.
farms.notable: A newer estate making a name at Best of Panama.

farms.name: Mount Totumas (De La Rosa)
farms.people: Jeff & Michael Dietrich; Karin de la Rosa (coffee lead), Alma Dietrich
farms.note: Tierras Altas (west of Volcán toward Santa Clara), Chiriquí, Panama — a 140-ha private cloud-forest reserve and off-grid eco-lodge bordering La Amistad International Park, just kilometres from the Costa Rican border. Founded in 2008 by brothers Jeff and Michael Dietrich, who reforested 30 ha of former cow pasture with native shade trees; mentored by the Hartmann family, they planted their first 500 Geisha trees in 2014 and now tend ~10,000 trees. Coffee is led by Jeff's daughter Karin de la Rosa under an ecology-first, biodiversity-driven philosophy. Won 1st place Geisha Natural at Best of Panama 2023 (96.25 pts, ~US$2,504/kg) and placed 6th in 2024. Washed, natural and experimental lots.
farms.notable: Won 1st place Geisha Natural at Best of Panama 2023 (96.25 pts, ~US$2,504/kg) and placed 6th in 2024.

farms.name: Finca Nuguo (Café Gallardo)
farms.note: Jurutungo, Renacimiento, Chiriquí, Panama — nestled against La Amistad International Park (Central America's largest rainforest reserve) on the Costa Rican border. Café Gallardo began when José Gallardo's father planted coffee in the region in 1960; in 2006 José "Pocho" Gallardo Araúz (who holds a PhD in civil engineering and applies it to processing and drying) saw Finca Nuguo's potential and planted Geisha. Nuguo's cold nights, high wind and heavy rainfall give low yields but intense flavour. José was the 2021 Best of Panama overall winner and a repeat placer; the family runs lower-altitude farms (La Santa, Zambrano, Guisado, Santa Clara) in traditional varieties, with Nuguo as the flagship Geisha. Washed, natural and long-fermented lots, micro-separated by day and plot, dried 3–6 weeks on covered raised beds.
farms.notable: Run by José “Pocho” Gallardo, the 2021 Best of Panama overall winner and a repeat competition placer.

farms.name: Finca El Injerto
farms.note: La Libertad, Huehuetenango. Coffee since ~1900, run by the 3rd/4th-generation Aguirre family. One of Central America’s most decorated farms with eight Cup of Excellence wins; first Guatemalan farm to earn Carbon Neutral certification (2017). Famous Pacamara and Gesha micro-lots.
farms.notable: One of Central America’s most decorated farms, with eight Cup of Excellence wins and record-setting Pacamara and Geisha lots.

farms.name: Finca El Socorro
farms.note: Palencia, near Antigua. 19th-century roots; the 3rd-generation De La Cerda family transformed it into a CoE powerhouse with multiple 1st-place Cup of Excellence wins. Renowned Gesha and exotic varietals across diverse microclimates.
farms.notable: The De La Cerda family built it into a CoE powerhouse with multiple 1st-place Cup of Excellence wins.

farms.name: Bella Vista (Finca Santa Clara)
farms.note: Antigua. Over a century of Zelaya-family heritage; 4th-generation agronomist Luis Pedro Zelaya manages farm-to-export traceability. Multiple Cup of Excellence and Genuine Antigua-certified micro-lots; the Bella Vista mill is a regional benchmark.
farms.notable: A Genuine Antigua-certified estate with multiple Cup of Excellence micro-lots from the volcanic slopes around Antigua, Guatemala.

farms.name: Finca La Bolsa
farms.people: María Elena Vides & Renardo "Nayo" Ovalle Vides (Vides 58)
farms.note: La Libertad, Huehuetenango (~1,300–1,600 m). A 108-ha estate founded 1958 by Dr Jorge Vides and now run by his daughter María Elena Vides and grandson Renardo "Nayo" Ovalle under the "Vides 58" name. A Cup of Excellence farm (2002) and one of Guatemala's most recognised specialty families, known for community programs and a quality-first ethos.
farms.notable: A 2002 Cup of Excellence farm and one of Guatemala’s most recognised specialty families, known for its community programs and meticulous processing.

farms.name: Finca Huixoc
farms.note: San Pedro Necta, Huehuetenango. A historic hacienda (founded 1911) with full on-site milling; one of the consistently top Huehue coffees, often ranked beside El Injerto.

farms.name: Finca Filadelfia
farms.note: Antigua (coffee since 1870). A historic Antigua estate with Genuine Antigua certification; classic chocolate-and-spice Antigua cup, plus tourism and on-site processing.

farms.name: Finca Santa Felisa
farms.note: Acatenango. A research-minded estate experimenting widely with varietals and processing (incl. Gesha, Pacamara, anaerobic and natural lots); a recurring competition and direct-trade name.

farms.name: Don Mayo Micro Mill
farms.people: Héctor Bonilla Cruz & Pablo Bonilla (Bonilla family)
farms.note: León Cortés / Tarrazú. Founded 2003 by Héctor Bonilla Cruz and his sons — one of Costa Rica's oldest and most respected micromills and a pioneer of the farm-to-mill micromill movement. Processes the family's own farms (La Loma, La Ladera, Bella Vista) plus neighbours' cherry; first place in the Costa Rica Cup of Excellence in 2009 and 2020.
farms.notable: Founded in 2003 by Héctor Bonilla Cruz and his sons, one of Costa Rica’s oldest and most respected micromills and a pioneer of Tarrazú honey processing.

farms.name: Finca Volcán Azul
farms.note: West Valley. A fifth-generation farm known for extended anaerobic fermentations and a repeat Cup of Excellence placer (e.g. CoE #2, 2017; later top-ranked anaerobic lots).
farms.notable: A fifth-generation farm and repeat Cup of Excellence placer, known for extended anaerobic ferments.

farms.name: Finca Las Nubes (Ureña)
farms.note: Los Santos. Took 1st place Honey/Natural at the 2026 Costa Rica Cup of Excellence with a Geisha (91.06), processed at the Roger Ureña Hidalgo mill.
farms.notable: Took 1st place Honey/Natural at the 2026 Costa Rica Cup of Excellence with a Geisha (91.06), processed at the Roger Ureña Hidalgo mill.

farms.name: Hacienda Aquiares
farms.note: Turrialba, founded 1890 — Costa Rica's largest and one of its oldest estates (~924 ha, an 1,800-person community), carbon-neutral and a Rainforest Alliance pioneer. Managed by Diego Robelo, who has driven varietal innovation (WCR trials) and is one of the few growing the rare F1 hybrids Esperanza and Centroamericano at commercial scale.
farms.notable: Founded in 1890 in Turrialba, Costa Rica’s largest and one of its oldest estates — a carbon-neutral, ~924-hectare farm supporting a community of around 1,800 people.

farms.name: Finca Santa Rosa
farms.note: Chalatenango. Founded 1979; shifted from pine to Pacamara in 2003. Won the 2019 El Salvador Cup of Excellence (honey Pacamara, 90.57) — a posthumous victory that lifted Chalatenango's coffee scene.
farms.notable: Won the 2019 El Salvador Cup of Excellence (honey Pacamara, 90.57) — a posthumous victory that lifted Chalatenango's coffee scene.

farms.name: Finca La Pacaya (MAVCOFFEE)
farms.note: Apaneca-Ilamatepec. Geisha from seeds acquired in Panama in 2013; multiple CoE awards including 1st place 2025 for a natural Geisha.
farms.notable: Multiple CoE awards including 1st place 2025 for a natural Geisha.

farms.name: Finca La Ilusión / Las Brumas
farms.note: Apaneca-Ilamatepec. The Menéndez family's multiple-CoE-winning farms; their SL28 from Las Brumas fuelled a World Barista Championship win (Dale Harris, 2017).
farms.notable: The Menéndez family’s multiple Cup of Excellence-winning farms, among El Salvador’s most respected specialty producers.

farms.name: Finca El Carmen
farms.note: Ataco, Apaneca-Ilamatepec, El Salvador (~1,300 m). Owned by the Alfaro family for over a century and now run by Fernando Alfaro, the fourth generation; founded mid-19th century by Antonio José Alfaro. Meticulous lot separation — Red, Orange and Pink Bourbon picked and processed separately — with sun-drying on clay patios.

farms.name: Finca La Salsa
farms.note: Santa Bárbara (planted 2018). A five-time Cup of Excellence winner (incl. two 1st places) and a mentorship hub — Benjamin Paz shares seeds and fermentation know-how, lifting many Santa Bárbara producers to auction.
farms.notable: A five-time Cup of Excellence winner.

farms.name: Finca Karmél
farms.note: San Luis Planes, Santa Bárbara. A family farm ('Garden of God') that took 3rd place in the Cup of Excellence; balanced, honey-and-stone-fruit cups.
farms.notable: A family farm whose name means “Garden of God,” a 3rd-place Cup of Excellence winner.

farms.name: Desarrollos Santa Lucía (Mierisch)
farms.note: Comayagua. Won the 2019 Honduras Cup of Excellence with a washed Geisha (94.84); part of the renowned Mierisch family's multi-country specialty operation.
farms.notable: Won the 2019 Honduras Cup of Excellence with a washed Geisha scoring 94.84, part of the Mierisch family’s estates.

farms.name: Fazenda Santa Inês
farms.note: Carmo de Minas, Mantiqueira. A historic, repeatedly CoE-winning estate famed for Yellow Bourbon; one of Brazil's benchmark fine-cup farms.
farms.notable: A historic, repeatedly Cup of Excellence-winning Brazilian estate famed for its exceptional Yellow Bourbon.

farms.name: Fazenda Rio Verde
farms.note: Mantiqueira de Minas. A 19th-century estate and CoE competitor; its Geisha won the Experimental category at Cup of Excellence Brazil 2025.
farms.notable: A 19th-century Brazilian estate and consistent Cup of Excellence competitor.

farms.name: Fazenda Samambaia
farms.note: Santo Antônio do Amparo, Sul de Minas. A long-respected estate and frequent competition name; clean, sweet, chocolate-and-nut Sul de Minas profiles.

farms.name: Finca Las Alasitas
farms.note: Caranavi. Part of the Rodríguez family's Sol de la Mañana program; their Geisha micro-lots are among Bolivia's highest-scoring and most awarded.
farms.notable: A celebrated high-altitude Bolivian micro-estate.

farms.name: Finca Java / Los Rodríguez lots
farms.note: Caranavi. Agricafe's farm network revitalising Bolivian specialty through the Sol de la Mañana producer-training program; notable Java-variety lots.

farms.name: Alpes Andinos cooperative
farms.note: Cajamarca highlands. A cooperative producing increasingly awarded washed lots (incl. Geisha); a fixture of Peru's rising Cup of Excellence presence.
farms.notable: A fixture of Peru's rising Cup of Excellence presence.

farms.name: Cooperativa Pangoa
farms.note: Junín. A long-running, women-and-organics-focused cooperative; a recognised name in Peruvian specialty and Fairtrade circuits.

farms.name: Finca El Limoncillo (Fincas Mierisch)
farms.note: Matagalpa. A flagship of the Mierisch family's Nicaraguan estates, internationally celebrated for its Java-variety lots and meticulous separation by plot and altitude.
farms.notable: A flagship of the Mierisch family's Nicaraguan estates, internationally celebrated for its Java-variety lots and meticulous separation.

farms.name: Finca Los Alpes
farms.note: Nueva Segovia. Won the 2023 Nicaragua Cup of Excellence with a natural Geisha; blackcurrant, mango and peach character from a distinctive microclimate.
farms.notable: Won the 2023 Nicaragua Cup of Excellence with a natural Geisha.

farms.name: Finca San José (Jinotega)
farms.note: Jinotega. Representative of Jinotega's balanced, characterful estates; a region producing the bulk of Nicaragua's higher-grown specialty.

farms.name: Hambela Washing Stations
farms.note: Guji. A celebrated cluster of washing stations known for vivid, fruit-forward naturals and clean washed lots; among the most sought-after Guji profiles.
farms.notable: A celebrated cluster of Guji washing stations known for vivid, fruit-forward naturals and crisp, clean washed lots.

farms.name: Aricha Washing Station
farms.note: Yirgacheffe. A well-known washing station famed for explosive blueberry/strawberry naturals — a benchmark for the Ethiopian natural style.

farms.name: Limu / Jimma cooperatives
farms.note: Western Ethiopia. Washed Limu and predominantly natural Jimma/Djimmah; rounded, low-acid, balanced cups distinct from the southern Yirgacheffe/Guji profile.

farms.name: Tegu Factory
farms.note: Murang'a. A respected washing station producing the classic Murang'a profile — bold blackcurrant, tomato and citrus over a heavy body.

farms.name: Kainamui Factory
farms.note: Kirinyaga. A consistently high-scoring factory; intense, sweet-savoury Kirinyaga lots with blackcurrant, plum and bright citric acidity.
farms.notable: A perennially top-scoring Kirinyaga factory, a Kenyan reference name.

farms.name: Gaturiri Factory
farms.note: Embu. A washing station turning out clean, juicy, berry-and-citrus Embu coffees; a lesser-known but high-quality Kenyan source.

farms.name: Royal Bean Geisha Estate
farms.note: Alishan, Chiayi. Described as the largest Gesha estate in Taiwan, with a full post-harvest mill and precise washed processing. Won the inaugural Taiwan competition (washed Gesha, 89.59, sold at US$183.50/lb in the 2022 Private Collection) and multiple top COE Taiwan placements; a 2025 Presidential Award winner.
farms.notable: Won the inaugural Taiwan competition (washed Gesha, 89.59, sold at US$183.50/lb in the 2022 Private Collection) and multiple top COE.

farms.name: Chia-Jung Chuang Estate
farms.note: Alishan misty forest. Gesha grown under natural canopy with cool nights and wide day-night swings; a clean, elegant, terroir-driven cup and a Cup of Excellence Taiwan name.
farms.notable: A clean, elegant, terroir-driven cup and a Cup of Excellence Taiwan name.

farms.name: Melastoma Estate (Bao-Si Huang)
farms.note: Chiayi highlands. A long-time tea farmer applying tea-like discipline to Gesha cultivation and honey processing; refined, sweet, floral cups and a COE Taiwan competitor.
farms.notable: Refined, sweet, floral cups and a COE Taiwan competitor.

farms.name: Nita Cafe
farms.note: Hualien, eastern Taiwan. A family farm in a topographically diverse region (mountains meeting the ocean); part of Taiwan's rising small-volume, high-quality estate scene.

farms.name: Lu Gao Coffee Estate (鹿篙咖啡莊園)
farms.note: Yuchi Township, Nantou County, central Taiwan — a 10-hectare estate run by the Taiwan Agricultural & Industrial Corporation (TAIC), a company with roots in the Japanese colonial-era tea trade. Around 13,850 coffee trees grow at ~700 m under shade trees such as Taiwan incense cedar, spanning Typica, SL34, Catuai, Villa Sarchi and Geisha. Built first and foremost as a growing estate, with rigorous soil-and-water conservation and processing standards.
farms.notable: A 10-hectare Nantou estate under the century-old TAIC, planting ~13,850 trees across Typica, SL34, Catuai, Villa Sarchi and Geisha.

farms.name: Discovery Coffee Farm
farms.note: Northern Thailand. A precision-focused estate where every stage is managed by the owner; premium Gesha and Java grown under natural shade — a Cup of Excellence Thailand competitor.
farms.notable: Premium Gesha and Java grown under natural shade — a Cup of Excellence Thailand competitor.

farms.name: Saenchai Estate
farms.note: Set in Asia's largest pine forest with a year-round cool climate; shade-grown coffee under mature canopy. A Cup of Excellence Thailand name reflecting the country's rising specialty scene.
farms.notable: A Cup of Excellence Thailand name reflecting the country's rising specialty scene.

farms.name: Finca Campo Hermoso
farms.note: Circasia, Quindío. A ~15–25 ha fourth-generation family farm run by Edwin Noreña, an agro-industrial engineer, Q-grader and Cup of Excellence judge nicknamed “El Alquimista” (the alchemist). A coffee R&D lab famous for layered, multi-stage fermentations and co-ferments — using “mossto” (recycled cherry must) plus hops, chili, and fresh/dehydrated fruit. Released under his El Alquimista brand and used in barista competitions worldwide.
farms.notable: A ~15–25 ha fourth-generation family farm run by Edwin Noreña, an agro-industrial engineer, Q-grader and Cup of Excellence judge.

farms.name: Finca El Paraíso
farms.note: Piendamó / Bolívar, Cauca. One of the world's most famous processing laboratories, run by Diego Bermúdez — a pioneer of scientifically-controlled fermentation: bioreactors, selected microorganisms, ozone-disinfected cherries, and the signature “thermal shock” washed method. Globally sought-after “designed” profiles (lychee, peach candy) sold under the Hachi/Letty lines.
farms.notable: One of the world's most famous processing laboratories, run by Diego Bermúdez — a pioneer of scientifically-controlled fermentation.

farms.name: Café Granja La Esperanza
farms.note: Valle del Cauca (since 1945). A pioneering producer-exporter across Potosí, Cerro Azul, Las Margaritas and a Panama farm; 50+ international awards, a WBC presence, and the founder of Colombia's first private coffee auction (2021). Famed for benchmark Geisha and bubblegum-sweet experimental lots.
farms.notable: A pioneering Colombian producer-exporter spanning the Potosí, Cerro Azul and Las Margaritas farms, plus a sister estate in Panama.

farms.name: Finca Monteblanco
farms.note: San Adolfo / Acevedo, Huila. A family farm (Aromas del Sur group) and a famous processing innovator — one of the original homes of Pink Bourbon and the near-proprietary Purple Caturra. Known for cultured ferments, long cold fermentations and co-infused lots that double as competition and year-round café coffees.
farms.notable: Rodrigo Sánchez's renowned Huila farm and a competition-circuit fixture.

farms.name: Hacienda El Obraje
farms.note: Tangua, Nariño. A high-altitude (~2,200 m) estate that transitioned to specialty over the 2010s; respected washed and natural Geisha, Caturra and Castillo, and a stop on the US/World Coffee Championship origin trips.
farms.notable: Respected washed and natural Geisha, Caturra and Castillo, and a stop on the US/World Coffee Championship origin trips.

farms.name: Peñas Blancas (processing center)
farms.note: A laboratory-minded processing center documented for open-tank oxidation, thermal shock and tightly logged drying schedules; its coffees appear in high-profile competitions and selective-roaster offerings.

farms.name: Fazenda Ambiental Fortaleza (FAF)
farms.note: Mococa/Caconde. The farm behind Brazil’s sustainable/biodynamic specialty movement — the Croce family returned in 2001, slashed coffee from most of the estate and rewilded it. A pioneer of selective raised-bed naturals and the anchor of a satellite microlot community (the “Bob-o-link” / FAF growers network).
farms.notable: A pioneer of selective raised-bed naturals and the anchor of a satellite microlot community (the “Bob-o-link” / FAF growers network).

farms.name: Sítio (Serra do Cigano) — João Hamilton
farms.note: Caconde. A smallholder mentored within the FAF community who built a centralised hilltop processing site, “Serra do Cigano” (Gypsy Hill); his highly selective raised-bed naturals helped spark a satellite microlot movement across the watershed.

farms.name: Port of Mokha
farms.note: Across remote Yemeni villages. Founded by Mokhtar Alkhanshali (subject of Dave Eggers’ “The Monk of Mokha”), a social-entrepreneur producer-exporter who pays growers roughly 3× open-market rates and helped reverse the decline of Yemeni coffee quality; co-founder of the Mokha Institute.
farms.notable: The Yemeni project behind record-setting auction prices and a bestselling origin story.

farms.name: Kerchanshe Trading
farms.note: Sidama/Guji/Yirgacheffe. Founded 2005 by Israel Degfa; the largest coffee producer and exporter in Ethiopia, working with over a million farmers and a major force in washed and natural lots across the south.
farms.notable: Ethiopia's largest specialty exporter, founded by Israel Degfa.

farms.name: Daye Bensa Coffee Export
farms.note: Bensa, Sidama. A prominent producer-exporter working closely with local farmers; celebrated for high-scoring Bensa naturals and washed lots (Keramo, Shantawene, Bombe) that feature on top-roaster menus worldwide.
farms.notable: Celebrated for high-scoring Bensa naturals and washed lots (Keramo, Shantawene, Bombe) that feature on top-roaster menus worldwide.

farms.name: Iris Estate
farms.people: Jamison Savage, Sasa Sestic & Elvin Siew
farms.note: Volcán, Panama. A super-specialty collaboration between Jamison Savage (Finca Deborah / Morgan Estate), 2015 World Barista Champion Sasa Sestic (Ona Coffee / Project Origin) and Elvin Siew. Coffee trees were planted within the existing forest without clearing the landscape, and the project is built around terroir-driven processing and meticulous carbonic-maceration work. Named lots (Aril, Enigma, Ambrosia, Odyssey, Vivid) have won barista-championship recognition.
farms.notable: A super-specialty collaboration between Jamison Savage and 2015 World Barista Champion Sasa Sestic.

farms.name: Finca La Huerta
farms.note: El Cielito mountain, Santa Bárbara. A Cup of Excellence winner (2013) on a mountain that has produced many of Honduras’ top COE lots; the farm whose neighbour Sasa Sestic bought, sparking his Project Origin work in Honduras.
farms.notable: A 2013 Cup of Excellence winner on a Honduran mountain that has produced many of the country’s top competition lots.

farms.name: Finca Beti
farms.note: El Cielito, Santa Bárbara. World Barista Champion Sasa Sestic’s own Honduran farm (neighbouring Finca La Huerta), with a quality lab and guest house; a base for Project Origin’s controlled-fermentation and carbonic-maceration experiments.
farms.notable: World Barista Champion Sasa Sestic’s own Honduran farm, neighbouring Finca La Huerta, complete with a quality lab and guest house.

farms.name: Finca El Árbol
farms.note: Nicaragua. One of Project Origin’s three owned farms (with Iris Estate in Panama and Finca Beti in Honduras); a site for terroir-driven processing experiments.

farms.name: Morgan Estate
farms.note: Volcán, Panama. One of Jamison Savage’s super-specialty Geisha farms (alongside Finca Deborah and Iris Estate), known for meticulous, innovative carbonic-maceration and natural processing.

farms.name: Cafetalera Herbazú
farms.note: Lourdes de Naranjo, West Valley. A pioneering micro-mill founded out of the 2001 coffee crisis and a 10-time Cup of Excellence winner; credited with inspiring the 200+ micro-mill movement across Costa Rica.
farms.notable: A pioneering Costa Rican micromill born out of the 2001 coffee crisis and a ten-time Cup of Excellence winner.

farms.name: Finca La Granadilla
farms.note: Santa María de Dota, Tarrazú. A third-generation farm and CoE “Legends”-recognised producer; bright, complex Tarrazú cup from careful fermentation and drying protocols.
farms.notable: A third-generation Costa Rican farm recognised in the Cup of Excellence “Legends” programme.

farms.name: Buku Washing Station
farms.note: Buku kebele, Hambela, West Guji — among the highest coffee in Ethiopia (~2,350 m). A sought-after cherry source (Buku Abel/Hurufa/Sisa stations) where slow maturation yields dense, small, intensely fruity beans from JARC 74110/74112/74158. Naturals and anaerobic naturals.

farms.name: Finca Los Pinos
farms.note: Matagalpa, Nicaragua. An organic farm run by Byron Corrales, one of the best-known Maracaturra specialists — producing washed, honey and natural Maracaturra microlots prized for clean, fruit-forward cups.

farms.name: El Cambalache & La Esperanza (Zavala)
farms.note: Dipilto, Nueva Segovia. Two farms run by third-generation producer Samuel Zavala, who won the 2025 Nicaragua Cup of Excellence. Planted to rare varieties (Sidra, Chiroso, Geisha, Bourbon Rosado, Maracaturra) with unconventional processing.
farms.notable: Two farms run by third-generation producer Samuel Zavala, who won the 2025 Nicaragua Cup of Excellence.

farms.name: La Brujufinca
farms.note: Pluma Hidalgo, Oaxaca — within the protected "Pluma" denomination of origin. A cloud-forest farm producing sweet, elegant, milk-chocolate-toned cups, including competition Geisha and the heritage Pluma Hidalgo (Typica) variety.

farms.name: Manto Niebla
farms.note: Pluma Hidalgo, Oaxaca. A newer mountaintop estate (planted from 2016, ~70 ha) helping revive Oaxacan specialty; grows Geisha, Marsellesa and the heritage Pluma Hidalgo Typica.

farms.name: Fazenda Guariroba
farms.people: Paiva & Lamounier family (Gabriel Lamounier, Homero Aguiar Paiva)
farms.note: Santo Antônio do Amparo, Campo das Vertentes. A five-generation family farm and a renowned fermentation specialist — Gabriel Lamounier ferments with native microbiota (no introduced strains) using meticulous protocols. Won the 2016 Cup of Excellence (Brazil Natural, 90.5) and has placed repeatedly since; grows 10+ varieties.
farms.notable: A five-generation family farm and renowned native-microbiota fermentation specialist.

farms.name: Kiangoi Factory (Rung'eto)
farms.note: Kirinyaga, on the southern slopes of Mt Kenya. A wet mill of the Rung'eto FCS (with Kii and Karimikui), serving ~950–1,150 smallholders. Classic clean, layered, fruit-driven Kenyan washed coffees; an International Taste Award winner.
farms.notable: An International Taste Award winner.

farms.name: Alvaro Vargas
farms.note: Urrao, Antioquia, Colombia. A small, meticulously kept garden-farm of Chiroso run by Álvaro Vargas, who comes from a coffee and cattle-ranching family and has emerged as a competition finalist since 2025. Washed Chiroso with dulce de leche, ripe citrus and sugar-cane sweetness; long open-tank fermentation and sun-bed drying.

farms.name: Isaac Espinoza
farms.note: Inkawasi, Cusco, Peru. One of the first to establish a coffee plantation and beneficio in Inkawasi, inspiring a district now making a name in specialty. Grows SL9 ("Gesha Inca") — a double-fermented washed lot with silky berries, melon, golden grape and coffee blossom.

farms.name: Nkanda Washing Station
farms.note: Ngozi, Burundi. A washing station that has built a strong local reputation for quality; lots are separated by hill and multigenerational family. Double-fermented washed Red Bourbon (12h wet + 12h in cement tanks) dried 20–25 days on raised beds — tea-like, with dried cherry, citrus and persimmon.

farms.name: Imani Estate
farms.note: Kiambu, Kenya. A ~500-acre estate (280 acres in forest, dams and marshland) on deep red volcanic Nitisol/Andosol soils, with a 16-lagoon water-recirculation system. AA washed separations of SL34 (and various) prized for raspberry, cherry and wild-rose clarity.

farms.name: Busambo (Umoco Washing Station)
farms.note: Busambo community, Kayanza, Burundi. Smallholders carry cherry on foot to the Umoco washing station ("light" in Kirundi). Washed Red Bourbon — double fermentation in wet and cement tanks, 20–25 days on raised beds — sparkling, floral and clean with cranberry, toffee and orange peel.

farms.name: Erick Bravo (Finca El Chaferote)
farms.note: San Agustín, Huila, Colombia. Finca El Chaferote sits at the edge of the Magdalena River, whose cool air helps cherries mature evenly. Erick Bravo is a Flowerchild favourite who places highly in Colombia's Cup of Excellence and Huila Mágico; his focus is Pink Bourbon, double-fermented (48h in cherry + 36h dry) and greenhouse-dried.
farms.notable: A Flowerchild favourite who places highly in Colombia’s Cup of Excellence and the Huila Mágico competition.

farms.name: Astrid Medina (Finca Buenavista)
farms.note: Planadas, Tolima, Colombia. Astrid Medina is one of Colombia's most celebrated producers — a Cup of Excellence winner (2015) known for immaculate, consistent processing. Her washed Gesha is a benchmark, silky and layered with dark fruit, boysenberry and honeysuckle.
farms.notable: Astrid Medina is one of Colombia's most celebrated producers — a Cup of Excellence winner (2015) known for immaculate, consistent.

farms.name: Terrazas del Pisque
farms.note: Pichincha, Quito, Ecuador. A 39-ha regenerative farm (17 ha in coffee, ~10,000 trees) managed by Sebastián Garcés with Arnaud Causse — an incubator of exotic varieties using chickens, sheep, bees and home-made biochar in place of chemical inputs. Clean, lively washed lots across Pacamara, Bourbon, Sidra, Java and the local Las Tolas.

farms.name: Luis Cuellar (Finca La Pradera)
farms.note: Santa María, Huila, Colombia. A 6-ha farm (3.5 ha in coffee, ~13,000 trees) run by agronomist Luis Enrique Cuellar and his brother Robinson — Luis manages plots and commercialisation, Robinson the processing. Pickers paid ~40% above average for ripe-only selection. Known for fruit-forward Pink Bourbon.

farms.name: Albeiro Ramirez
farms.note: Urrao, Antioquia, Colombia. A lifelong coffee farmer recognised for meticulous, standardised, traceable processing. His two-stage fermented washed Chiroso is distinctively buttery and chocolate-weighted — a Flowerchild standout from Urrao.

farms.name: Goro Bedessa Washing Station
farms.note: Goro Bedessa, Hambela Wamena, Guji, Ethiopia. A high-altitude (~2,100 m) washing station run by Ismael Hassen, drawing cherry from several hundred Guji Oromo smallholders. Consistently among Flowerchild's top-scoring Ethiopians — washed, with elderberry, blueberry, black tea and jasmine florals.

farms.name: Jose Flores (Finca El Silencio)
farms.note: Urrao, Antioquia, Colombia. A family farm worked by Jose Flores and his wife Marta, meticulous about drying. His washed Chiroso uses a ~6-day water-changed concrete-tank ferment, giving a bigger-bodied, dark-berry, vanilla-and-cane-sugar profile.

farms.name: Duvan Calderon
farms.note: Salado Blanco, Huila, Colombia. A third-generation producer (28) who took over after his father's passing and runs the farm with his sister, specialising in Pink Bourbon (Bourbon Rosado). Temperature-controlled oxidation ferment under 45°C; rich, full-bodied, balanced cup.

farms.name: Sookoo Tima
farms.note: Odo Shakiso, Guji, Ethiopia. A group effort from the kebeles of Tima coordinated by Ture Waji, drying Gibrinna 74110 and Serto 74112 on raised beds at ~2,173 m in thin layers. Vibrant, pristine washed cup — blackberry, citrus, nectarine, floral.

farms.name: Telila Washing Station
farms.note: Gure Kesso, Jimma, western Ethiopia. A newer private washing station run by Mike Mamo's team, producing an atypical Jimma profile — big, viscous, creamy, with wild honey, orange and papaya rather than the usual tea-like berry. Predominantly 74112 landrace field blend.

farms.name: Buncho Washing Station
farms.note: Bensa, Sidama, Ethiopia. A washing station owned by Asefa Dukamo Korma (Daye Bensa) drawing on Shantawene producers, pooling primarily Landrace 74158 plus native garden varieties. Exceptionally clean honey processing — wild-hive honey, white nectarine, navel orange.

farms.name: Oscar Garcia (Finca San Isidro)
farms.note: Santa Clara, San Agustín, Huila, Colombia. A young San Agustín producer whose washed Gesha is intensely tropical — mangosteen, lychee, kumquat, Angel's-Trumpet florals. 48h underwater ferment, raised-bed dried.

farms.name: Brayan Joven (Finca La Esperanza)
farms.note: Timaná, Huila, Colombia. A 2.5-ha, 6,000-tree third-generation farm. Brayan uses a striking staggered harvest (picking Mon–Fri, washing all batches together Friday) to build a 12–96h fermentation gradient; works alongside his father's and brother's nearby farms.

farms.name: David Ruben Gomez (Finca El Refugio)
farms.note: Vereda La Florida, La Plata, Huila, Colombia. A ~4-ha farm run by David Gomez, a 20-year coffee veteran from a large coffee-growing family moving into specialty. Full-bodied, stewed-fruit washed Gesha with marmalade, persimmon and panela.

farms.name: Finca Soledad (Pepe Jijon)
farms.note: Intag valley, Imbabura, Ecuador — a 120-ha farm (only 5 ha in coffee) inside a UNESCO World Heritage GEOPARK, founded 2010. Pepe Jijon is a celebrated biodynamic producer whose “Nature Wave” philosophy minimises stress on the bean; rainwater-only, indirect-dried washed Gesha of remarkable cleanliness.
farms.notable: A celebrated Ecuadorian biodynamic producer whose “Nature Wave” philosophy minimises stress on the coffee plant and bean.

farms.name: Consuelo Rubio
farms.people: Consuelo Rubio Peralta & family (Richard, Roxana, Blanca Flor)
farms.note: Cajamarca, Peru. A family operation revived by Consuelo Rubio's children returning from the city after their father's passing; sealed-bag double fermentation and solar-tent drying. Known for round, floral Sidra and Tabi — a distinctive Peruvian take on these cultivars.

farms.name: German Peña (Finca El Roblar)
farms.note: Suaza, Huila, Colombia. German Peña built Finca El Roblar from a bare plot his father left him into a world-renowned operation, working with his wife, kids and extended family. His washed Pink Bourbon took 4th at Osito's Copa de Oro — a hyper-sweet strawberry-reduction profile with blackberry, Muscadine grape and passionfruit.
farms.notable: German Peña built Finca El Roblar from a bare plot his father left him into a world-renowned operation, farming alongside his wife and children.

farms.name: Jhon Didier (Finca Villa Flor)
farms.note: Urrao, Antioquia, Colombia. A fast-rising farmer on the Penderisco River growing lulo, avocado, plantains and coffee. His washed Chiroso (up to 4-day clean-water ferment, shade-tent dried) is clean and lightly fruited — dried blueberry, raisin, mandarin.

farms.name: Yesica Ale Llanqui
farms.note: Cusco, Peru. Yesica Llanqui Ale is a leading woman in her community and an advocate for women's participation in specialty coffee, with growing recognition across the Cusco region. Her washed Gesha (30h ferment, 16-day shaded-bed drying) is elegant and understated — white flowers, apricot, sweet lime, panela and pear, viscous yet clean.

farms.name: Familia Ccapa (Palanganayoc)
farms.note: Palanganayoc, Challabamba district, Cusco, Peru — Flowerchild's most remote source, about 10 hours from Cusco, where third-generation Typica trees from past generations still fruit.
